question_text: BM25 improves on plain term counting by considering which THREE factors?
options: Term frequency, inverse document frequency, and document length normalisation
Synonyms, embeddings, and click data
Recency, author, and popularity
Capitalisation, punctuation, and stopwords
explanation: Correct: BM25 ('Best Match 25') scores by how often the term appears in the document (TF), how rare it is across all documents (IDF), and normalises for document length so long docs don't win unfairly. Synonyms/embeddings belong to semantic search, and recency/popularity or punctuation signals are web-ranking or preprocessing concerns, not part of BM25.

question_text: What makes a system 'multimodal'?
options: It supports multiple users at once
It processes multiple input types — e.g., text plus images
It runs on multiple GPUs
It answers in multiple languages
explanation: Correct: 'modal' refers to input type — multimodal AI handles text + images (+ audio/video), like LLaVA (language+vision) or CLIP (image+text in one vector space). Concurrent users is a serving concern, multi-GPU is hardware, and multilinguality is still a single modality (text) — none of those are modalities.

question_text: What is LLaVA's architecture, per the source?
options: A CLIP vision encoder → projection layer → Llama/Vicuna LLM
An OCR engine feeding a keyword index
Two LLMs debating about the image
A diffusion model that redraws the image as text
explanation: Correct: LLaVA bolts CLIP's vision encoder onto a language model via a projection layer — image (base64) + text prompt in, detailed description out; the project uses it for charts, diagrams and tables at ~2-5s per image on the RTX 4060 Ti. It understands meaning rather than just extracting characters (not OCR), and there's no debate loop or diffusion involved.

question_text: How does an LLM 'see' an image in the LLaVA design?
options: The image is converted to a text caption first, and only the caption is used
The vision encoder's image vector is projected into the LLM's token space
Pixels are fed directly as tokens
The LLM requests the image bytes from disk
explanation: Correct: CLIP's ViT encodes the image into a 768-dim vector which a projection layer maps into the LLM's token embedding space — the image becomes 'tokens' the LLM can attend to. It's richer than caption-then-discard, raw pixels can't be consumed as text tokens directly, and models don't fetch files — the pipeline passes the encoded image in.

question_text: In 'CLIP ViT-B/32', what does the 32 refer to?
options: 32 attention layers
32×32-pixel patch size — the image is split into patches treated as tokens
32 million parameters
A 32-token output limit
explanation: Correct: a Vision Transformer splits the image into fixed-size pixel patches, each treated as a 'token' for self-attention; B = base model size and 32 = the patch dimension. The number doesn't count layers or parameters, and ViT produces embeddings, not token-limited text output.

question_text: Why did the project choose Vision AI (LLaVA) over OCR for document images?
options: OCR is too expensive to license
The goal was understanding charts and diagrams — their MEANING — not just extracting printed text
LLaVA is faster than OCR
OCR cannot run locally
explanation: Correct: OCR extracts text via pattern matching (fast, great for clean print) but can't describe a bar chart's trend or explain a flow diagram; LLaVA interprets meaning, which the RAG needed. OCR tools are widely free, LLaVA is much SLOWER (~2-5s/image) not faster, and OCR runs locally fine — capability, not licensing or speed, drove the choice.

question_text: Why does the pipeline resize images to 1024px BEFORE base64-encoding them for the Ollama API?
options: Ollama rejects non-square images
Base64 inflates size ~33%, so shrinking the image first minimises the transmission overhead
Base64 only works on small files
The GPU can't render larger images
explanation: Correct: base64 converts binary to ASCII for JSON/HTTP transport at ~33% size overhead — resizing to 1024px (also LLaVA's sweet spot) keeps the encoded payload small. Ollama doesn't demand square images, base64 has no file-size limit, and rendering isn't involved — it's about transfer efficiency.

question_text: Which behaviour separates an AI AGENT from a plain chat LLM?
options: It generates longer answers
It takes actions in a loop — calling tools and using their results — until the task is done
It has a bigger context window
It always refuses unsafe requests
explanation: Correct: an agent is autonomous (decides each step), goal-directed (works toward completion, not one reply), and tool-using (calls functions and consumes results) — 'what's the weather?' triggers a search rather than 'my training cut off in 2024.' Answer length, context size and safety behaviour are properties any chat model can have without being agentic.

question_text: In the ReAct loop, what runs immediately AFTER the agent's ACTION + INPUT are executed?
options: FINAL_ANSWER
OBSERVATION — the tool's result, which the next THOUGHT reasons about
A new user question
Model retraining
explanation: Correct: ReAct (Yao et al., 2022) alternates THOUGHT → ACTION → INPUT → OBSERVATION → THOUGHT..., with the tool result (OBSERVATION) feeding the next reasoning step until a FINAL_ANSWER. FINAL_ANSWER only comes when the agent has enough information, the user isn't re-prompted mid-loop, and no training happens at inference. 2026 note: production agents now use native tool-calling APIs, but the reason-act loop is the same.

question_text: Which part of a tool/skill definition does the LLM read to DECIDE when to use it?
options: The implementation code
The description of what it does
The unit tests
The function's return type
explanation: Correct: a skill = name + description + parameters + implementation, and the description is what the LLM reads to decide when the tool applies — write it for the model, not just for humans. The implementation and tests never enter the prompt, and return types aren't part of the decision text. 2026 note: MCP now standardises how these definitions are published to agents.

question_text: Which set of ingredients does the project's agent system prompt include?
options: Only the user's question
Current date/time, the skill list with examples, exact format rules, and behavioural rules
The model's full training data summary
Previous users' conversation logs
explanation: Correct: the system prompt injects the current date/time, every skill with description+params+example, the exact THOUGHT/ACTION/INPUT/FINAL_ANSWER format, and behavioural rules (cite sources, precise numbers) — prompt engineering is designing exactly this text. The user question alone gives no tooling context, training-data summaries don't exist as prompt artifacts, and other users' logs would be both useless and a privacy hole.

question_text: How does the project make the LLM's output reliably machine-parseable?
options: Temperature 0.1 plus explicit format rules, extracted with regex
A JSON parser that fixes any malformed output
Retraining the model on JSON files
Asking politely twice
explanation: Correct: structured output is achieved with near-deterministic sampling (0.1) + exact format instructions, then regex extraction of ACTION/INPUT/THOUGHT/FINAL_ANSWER. Parsers can't reliably repair arbitrary malformed text, retraining isn't needed for formatting, and repetition without format rules changes nothing. 2026 note: constrained decoding now GUARANTEES schema-valid output at the API level.

question_text: Why does Chain of Thought prompting improve accuracy on complex tasks?
options: It makes the model use more parameters
The model 'thinks out loud' step by step, so intermediate reasoning supports the final answer
It doubles the context window
It caches previous answers
explanation: Correct: CoT has the model explain reasoning step by step — the ReAct THOUGHT field implements it naturally — and writing out intermediate steps measurably improves complex-task accuracy. Parameter count and context size are fixed at inference, and caching is a separate system. 2026 note: reasoning models (o3, Claude extended thinking, DeepSeek R1) train this in as test-time compute.

question_text: How does a SEMANTIC cache differ from a regular (exact-match) cache?
options: It stores answers in RAM instead of disk
It matches new questions to stored ones by MEANING (embedding similarity), so paraphrases hit
It expires entries after one hour
It only caches one-word queries
explanation: Correct: 'What is ACID?' and 'Explain ACID properties in databases' are different strings — an exact-match dict lookup misses, but embeddings at cosine ≥0.92 hit, returning the cached answer in ~10ms instead of 10-120s of LLM time. The project's cache is disk-persistent (LanceDB), has no one-hour expiry (staleness is flagged, not deleted), and handles full questions.

question_text: What is the risk of LOWERING the semantic-cache similarity threshold below 0.92?
options: The cache stops matching anything
More cache hits, but a growing risk of returning the WRONG cached answer to a genuinely different question
Cache files grow larger
Embeddings become less accurate
explanation: Correct: the threshold balances false hits vs missed paraphrases — at 0.92, 'What is ACID?' matches 'Explain ACID properties' but NOT 'What is BASE?'; drop it and different questions start colliding with cached answers. Raising (not lowering) causes missed matches, storage size is unaffected by the threshold, and the embedding model's accuracy doesn't change — only the cutoff does.

question_text: Why is a cached answer to 'NIFTY closing price today' dangerous a month later, and what does the project do about it?
options: The embedding drifts over time; the cache re-embeds nightly
Time-sensitive answers go stale; regex-detected keywords (today/price/news/latest) trigger an age-based staleness warning
The cache auto-deletes financial questions
Nothing — cached answers are always trusted
explanation: Correct: semantic staleness means the cached text is still similar but the FACT is outdated; the project detects time-sensitive queries by keyword regex and shows warnings scaled to cache age ('⚠️ cached 7d ago... 🔴 35d ago'). Embeddings don't drift (vectors are static), entries aren't deleted by topic, and blind trust is exactly the failure being prevented.

question_text: Which events does the project's persistent memory cache survive?
options: App restarts and PC reboots — cleared only by explicit user action
Only page refreshes
Nothing — it's session-scoped
Restarts, but it empties every 24 hours
explanation: Correct: unlike LLM context (lost at session end), the cache lives in LanceDB (_cache.lance) with answers, sources, hit counts and timestamps — surviving restarts and reboots, deleted only via 'Clear memory' or deleting the project. That rules out session-scoped and refresh-only survival, and there is no periodic purge — staleness triggers warnings, not deletion.

question_text: Why are GPUs thousands of times faster than CPUs for neural networks?
options: GPUs have higher clock speeds
Neural nets are matrix multiplications, and GPUs run those in massive parallel across thousands of cores
GPUs have more cache memory
GPUs compress the model automatically
explanation: Correct: GPUs were built for parallel matrix operations (originally graphics) — the RTX 4060 Ti's 4,352 CUDA cores multiply matrices simultaneously, which is exactly what neural networks need (87 chunks/s embedding vs 4 on CPU). GPU clocks are typically LOWER than CPU clocks, cache size isn't the differentiator, and compression (quantization) is a software step, not a GPU feature.

question_text: What happens when a model doesn't fit in VRAM?
options: It refuses to load at all
It spills into system RAM and runs much slower
It automatically quantizes itself
VRAM expands using disk space
explanation: Correct: models must fit entirely in VRAM for full GPU speed; overflow spills to system RAM and slows everything down. Runtimes like Ollama still load and run oversized models (slowly) rather than refusing, quantization is a choice made when downloading/converting the model, and there's no automatic VRAM-to-disk expansion in this stack.

question_text: In PyTorch, what does model.to('cuda') do?
options: Compiles the model to C++
Moves the model's tensors onto the GPU so operations run on CUDA cores
Enables quantization
Connects to a cloud GPU service
explanation: Correct: CUDA is NVIDIA's parallel computing platform, and PyTorch uses it transparently — .to('cuda') transfers tensors to GPU memory so subsequent math runs on GPU cores. No compilation to C++ happens at that call, quantization is separate, and 'cuda' refers to the LOCAL NVIDIA device, not a cloud service.

question_text: GPU batch embedding (batch=256) achieved 87 chunks/s vs 4 chunks/s sequential on CPU. What explains the ~21x speedup?
options: The GPU model is smaller
256 chunks are processed simultaneously in one GPU forward pass instead of one at a time
The CPU was thermally throttled
Batching skips the embedding of similar chunks
explanation: Correct: batch processing feeds 256 chunks through the model in a single parallel forward pass — the GPU's thousands of cores work at once, versus sequential one-at-a-time CPU processing. The model is identical on both devices, no throttling was reported, and every chunk is still embedded — batching changes HOW they're processed, not how many.

question_text: The project uses 16 THREADS for PDF page extraction but separate PROCESSES for CPU embedding. Why the difference?
options: Threads are newer technology than processes
Page extraction is I/O-bound (GIL releases during I/O), while embedding is CPU-bound math that needs the GIL bypassed via separate processes
Processes share memory more easily
PDF libraries only work with threads
explanation: Correct: threads share memory cheaply and genuinely parallelise I/O waits (disk reads), but Python's GIL serialises CPU work — so CPU-heavy embedding uses ProcessPoolExecutor, each process with its own interpreter and GIL. Age of the technology is irrelevant, it's THREADS that share memory (processes don't — that's the point), and PDF libraries work under either model.

question_text: What does Python's GIL (Global Interpreter Lock) actually prevent?
options: More than one thread executing Python bytecode at the same time
Programs from using more than 1GB of RAM
Race conditions of any kind
Python from calling C libraries
explanation: Correct: the GIL lets only one thread run Python bytecode at a time, making threads useless for CPU-heavy parallelism (workarounds: multiprocessing, GPU, or C extensions like numpy that release the GIL). It has nothing to do with memory limits, it does NOT prevent all race conditions (only bytecode-level interleaving), and calling C libraries is allowed — many release the GIL while running. 2026 note: Python 3.13+ offers a free-threaded no-GIL build (PEP 703).

question_text: Why is extracting readable text from a PDF harder than from a plain text file?
options: PDFs are always encrypted
PDFs store text as positioned character objects, so reading order must be reconstructed by spatial analysis
PDF text is stored as images only
PDFs use a secret proprietary format
explanation: Correct: PDFs place characters at coordinates rather than storing flowing text, so extractors (PyMuPDF fastest/C-based, pypdf pure-Python fallback, pdfplumber for tables) must spatially reconstruct reading order. Encryption is optional and rare, only scanned PDFs are image-based, and the PDF spec is open — layout, not secrecy, is the challenge.

question_text: How does pdfplumber detect tables, and in what two formats does the project store them?
options: Machine-learning table classifier; stored as CSV and SQL
Horizontal/vertical line detection for boundaries; stored as markdown (readable) plus flat text (embeddable)
Font-size analysis; stored as images
Regular expressions on cell text; stored as JSON only
explanation: Correct: pdfplumber finds table boundaries via ruled-line detection, and each table is stored twice — markdown for human-readable citation and flat text for embedding. No ML classifier, font-size heuristic, or regex detection is described, and CSV/SQL/image storage isn't what the pipeline produces.

question_text: Besides extracting clean paragraph text, what does the project's BeautifulSoup HTML parsing track — and why?
options: JavaScript variables, to execute page logic
The h1→h2→h3 heading hierarchy, to assign section names to paragraphs
Image alt tags, to build the CLIP index
Cookie values, to keep sessions alive
explanation: Correct: after stripping noise (scripts, navigation, footers), the parser follows heading hierarchy so every paragraph inherits its section name — the same section-aware benefit PDFs get from heading detection. BeautifulSoup parses static HTML and never executes JavaScript, alt-tag harvesting isn't the described pipeline, and cookies are irrelevant to content extraction.

question_text: Which chunk would the project's noise filter REMOVE before embedding?
options: A 400-word explanation of latency
A 25-word fragment, or a chunk with six [N] citation markers
A markdown table of SLA values
A section heading with a full paragraph under it
explanation: Correct: the filter drops chunks under 30 words (too short to be meaningful) and citation-heavy chunks (>5 [N] markers — reference lists), plus wiki navigation patterns and DOI/ISBN strings; a Wikipedia page went from 54 raw to 30 clean chunks. Substantive explanations, tables, and heading+paragraph content are exactly what's KEPT — they carry real meaning.

question_text: What is Mermaid, in the project's export pipeline?
options: A Python plotting library that outputs PNGs
A text-based diagram language ('graph TD; A-->B') rendered as visual diagrams in the browser
A vector database for image chunks
An OCR post-processor
explanation: Correct: Mermaid turns text like 'graph TD → A[Box] --> B[Box2]' into rendered flowcharts (plus sequence, ER, class and timeline diagrams) via Mermaid.js from a CDN in HTML exports. It isn't a Python plotting library (that would be matplotlib), doesn't store anything (not a DB), and has nothing to do with OCR.

question_text: What mapping does an inverted index store?
options: Document → list of words it contains
Word → list of documents containing it
Vector → nearest neighbours
Query → cached answers
explanation: Correct: an inverted index maps each word to the documents containing it — 'inverted' because it flips the natural document→words direction, enabling instant keyword lookup inside LanceDB's FTS. Document→words is a forward index, vector→neighbours is what HNSW approximates, and query→answer mapping is the semantic cache.

question_text: In TF-IDF, why does the IDF part boost RARE terms?
options: Rare terms are usually spelled correctly
A term appearing in few documents discriminates better between documents than a term appearing everywhere
Rare terms compress better in the index
Frequent terms are always stopwords
explanation: Correct: IDF weights terms by rarity across the corpus — 'the' appears everywhere and identifies nothing, while 'HNSW' pinpoints relevant docs; TF then rewards repeated use within a document. Spelling and compression are irrelevant to relevance scoring, and frequent terms aren't all stopwords (domain words can be frequent) — IDF handles the spectrum smoothly rather than by stopword lists.

question_text: When does the project build its FTS index, and why there?
options: On every query, for freshness
Once after each ingestion — rebuilding per query cost ~500ms each time for identical results
Only at first install
Never — LanceDB indexes automatically on read
explanation: Correct: table.create_fts_index('text', replace=True) runs once per ingest; an earlier version rebuilt it inside hybrid_query, adding ~500ms to EVERY query for an index that hadn't changed — a classic 'move invariant work out of the hot path' fix. Per-query rebuilding is the bug, not the goal; new ingests after install still need indexing; and FTS indexing is explicit, not automatic on read.

question_text: Query expansion increases RECALL. What does recall measure, and what's the accepted cost?
options: Answer generation speed; costs more VRAM
The fraction of relevant documents actually retrieved; costs slight precision loss
Cache hit rate; costs disk space
Token throughput; costs latency only
explanation: Correct: recall = share of relevant documents you actually retrieved; running 3 query variants catches content phrased differently, at the cost of some precision (a few less-relevant results sneak in). Recall isn't about generation speed, cache rates, or throughput — those are different metrics entirely.

question_text: Which features does FastAPI provide for the project's RAG server?
options: Vector search algorithms
REST endpoints with automatic OpenAPI docs (e.g., @app.post('/query'))
GPU management
HTML page rendering for the main UI
explanation: Correct: FastAPI is the Python web framework exposing REST endpoints on port 8000 with auto-generated OpenAPI documentation — @app.post('/query') routes HTTP requests to smart_retrieve(). Vector search lives in LanceDB/retriever, GPU management is hardware.py + pynvml, and the browser UI is Gradio on port 7860, not FastAPI.

question_text: What does Gradio do in this stack?
options: Serves the REST API on port 8000
Converts Python functions into a browser UI (Textbox, Chatbot, Dataframe) on port 7860
Renders Mermaid diagrams to PNG
Schedules background ingestion jobs
explanation: Correct: Gradio is the ML-demo UI library — one line turns Python functions into web components; the app runs at port 7860. The REST API is FastAPI+uvicorn on port 8000, Mermaid renders in the browser via Mermaid.js (not through Gradio), and job scheduling isn't Gradio's role.

question_text: In what ORDER does RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter try split points?
options: Characters → words → sentences → paragraphs
Paragraph breaks (\n\n) → newlines → sentences ('. ') → spaces → characters
Random positions until chunks fit
Fixed 512-character cuts with no preference
explanation: Correct: it tries the most natural boundaries first — double newline, then newline, then sentence ends, then spaces, then raw characters as last resort — respecting sentence/paragraph structure before falling back. Starting with character-level cuts would be that order reversed — the most brutal split first — and random or fixed-position cuts are exactly what this splitter exists to avoid.

question_text: What role does PyArrow play for LanceDB?
options: It renders progress bars
It defines the columnar table schema (pa.schema/pa.field) for type-safe storage
It downloads models from HuggingFace
It encrypts the database files
explanation: Correct: PyArrow is the columnar data format library LanceDB uses for schema definition — pa.schema([pa.field('text', pa.string()), pa.field('text_vector', pa.list_(pa.float32(), 1024)), ...]) — enabling efficient columnar operations. Progress bars, model downloads (that's sentence-transformers/HF hub), and encryption are unrelated to its role here.

question_text: Why was DuckDuckGo Search chosen for the agent's web research?
options: It has the largest index of any engine
It offers a free, no-API-key Python library returning {title, href, body} results
It is the only engine allowed by Ollama
It caches results locally by default
explanation: Correct: duckduckgo_search's DDGS().text('query') needs no API key, costs nothing, and returns structured results — ideal for a fully local, no-account stack. Index size wasn't the criterion, Ollama has no opinion on search engines, and local caching is handled by the project's own semantic cache, not the search library.

question_text: How does the Live Metrics panel get GPU utilisation, VRAM, temperature and power data?
options: By parsing nvidia-smi's printed table every call
Via pynvml (NVIDIA Management Library bindings), sampled every second in a background thread
From Windows Task Manager's API
The GPU driver pushes events to Python
explanation: Correct: pynvml (nvidia-ml-py) provides programmatic real-time GPU metrics, and a background thread samples them every 1 second for the panel. Scraping nvidia-smi text output is the hacky alternative pynvml replaces, Task Manager exposes no such Python API, and NVML is polled — drivers don't push events to Python.

question_text: What does psutil contribute, and where does it run?
options: GPU-only metrics, NVIDIA cards only
Cross-platform system metrics — CPU %, RAM, frequency, battery — on Windows, Linux and macOS
Python package installation
Network packet capture
explanation: Correct: psutil supplies the CPU/RAM/frequency/battery side of the dashboard and works across all three OSes. GPU metrics come from pynvml (that's the NVIDIA-only one), package installation is pip's job, and packet capture is a different domain (e.g., scapy) entirely.

question_text: What is uvicorn's exact role relative to FastAPI?
options: It replaces FastAPI on Windows
It is the ASGI web server that accepts HTTP connections and routes them to FastAPI's handlers
It generates the OpenAPI documentation
It load-balances multiple GPUs
explanation: Correct: FastAPI defines the app and endpoints; uvicorn.run(app, host='0.0.0.0', port=8000) is the ASGI server that actually listens for HTTP and dispatches requests to those handlers — framework and server are separate layers. It complements (never replaces) FastAPI, the OpenAPI docs are FastAPI's own feature, and GPU balancing is unrelated to a web server.

question_text: When LanceDB replaced ChromaDB, only db.py changed. Which principle made that possible?
options: Test-driven development
Modular architecture — each module does one thing, so storage changes stay inside the storage module
Continuous deployment
Pair programming
explanation: Correct: the system is split into single-purpose modules (ingester/embedder/db/retriever/reranker/...) like kitchen appliances — replace the blender without touching the oven; swapping databases touched only db.py, and GPU support touched only embedder.py. TDD, CD and pairing are practices that don't by themselves contain change to one file — module boundaries do.

question_text: How does the Single Responsibility Principle define 'one responsibility'?
options: One function per file
Every module/class/function should have exactly ONE reason to change
No function longer than 10 lines
Each developer owns one module
explanation: Correct: SRP (the S in SOLID) says a unit should have exactly one reason to change — if a function does two things, split it into two functions. File counts, line limits and code ownership are style or process rules; 'one reason to change' is the actual principle.
